document: Introduction to Internet of Things Assignment-Week 5 Solutions

questions.questionText: What does the term "Interoperability" refer to in the context of IoT?
options: a. The ability of a system to operate without power
b. The ability of different systems/products to work together without restrictions
c. The ability to increase the speed of the internet
questions.explanation: Interoperability is a characteristic of a product or system whose interfaces are completely understood to work with other products or systems. (See lecture 21@ 05:40)

questions.questionText: In the collaborative concept (Cosine) theory for interoperability, an object is defined as a quadruple (A, B, C, D). What does 'C' represent?
options: d. The definition of the object
questions.explanation: In the Cosine quadruple (A, B, C, D), C represents the context of A. (See lecture 21 @ 21:16)

questions.questionText: Which of the following best describes the "Syntactic Interoperability" challenge?
options: a. Devices having different physical shapes
b. Devices using different message formats and data structures
c. Devices located in different countries
questions.explanation: Syntactic interoperability concerns the message formats and data structures (e.g., XML syntax) used for device interaction. (See lecture 21 @ 17:13)

questions.questionText: Which of the following is a primary reason for the popularity of Arduino in IoT implementations?
options: a) It is highly expensive and exclusive
b) It is low resource-consuming and cheap
c) It requires high-power external cooling
d) It only supports digital signals
questions.explanation: Arduino devices are popular because they are cheap, low resource-consuming, and open-source. (See lecture: 22 @01:10)

questions.questionText: What is the clock speed of the Arduino Uno board?
questions.explanation: The Arduino Uno operates at a voltage of 5V with a clock speed of 16 MHz. (See lecture: 22@07:01)

questions.questionText: Which function is used to iteratively loop over instances in an Arduino sketch?
questions.explanation: The loop() function is used for iteratively looping over instances, while setup() is used for initial configurations. (See lecture: 22@12:48)

questions.questionText: In Arduino, what is the default starting index for an array?
questions.explanation: Similar to C/C++, the conventional starting index for arrays in Arduino is zero. (See lecture: 23@04:16)

questions.questionText: What does the delay(5000) command do in a traffic signal simulation?
options: b) Introduces a delay of 5 seconds
c) Turns off all LEDs immediately
d) Skips the current execution loop
questions.explanation: The delay function takes input in milliseconds; therefore, 5000 milliseconds equals a 5-second delay. (See lecture: 23@15:30)

questions.questionText: Which header file must be called to use mathematical functions like sin() or cos()?
questions.explanation: To apply math functions, the math. h header must be initially called. (See lecture: 23@08:18)

questions.questionText: The DHT22 sensor is used for measuring which physical quantities?
questions.explanation: DHT stands for Digital Humidity and Temperature sensor. (See lecture: 24 @04:45)

questions.questionText: What is the recommended voltage range for the VCC pin of a DHT sensor to avoid damage?
questions.explanation: The supply should range from 3.3V to 5V; exceeding this may damage the sensor. (See lecture: 24 @05:57)

questions.questionText: When integrating a typical digital temperature and humidity sensor (like the DHT series) with an Arduino board, which Arduino pin connection is used to receive the sensor's data?
questions.explanation: In interfacing a digital temperature/humidity sensor with an Arduino, the signal/data pin is connected to a digital input pin on the Arduino - this pin carries the sensor's output readings to the microcontroller for processing. (See lecture: 24 @03:22)

questions.questionText: Which of the following describes the primary function of an actuator?
options: b. Converts energy or signals into motion
questions.explanation: Actuators are mechanical or electromechanical devices that convert energy or signals into motion. (See lecture 25 @ 02:04)

questions.questionText: When using the Servo library in Arduino, which function is used to specify the pin where the servo is connected?
questions.explanation: The attach() function is used to define the pin number to which the servo motor is connected (e.g., myservo.attach(9)). (See lecture 25 @ 08:16)

questions.questionText: In the context of the Arduino Servo library, which of the following is the correct function to command the servo motor to rotate to a specific angle?
questions.explanation: The write() function is used to control the position of the servo motor. It takes degree values (typically between 0 and 180) as an argument and rotates the motor to that specific angle. For example, servo.write(90) moves the servo to the 90-degree position. (See lecture 25 @9:57)
